teamObjective.explanation: Both teammates have small hands (player 0 has 4 cards, partner has 2). The immediate priority is to progress cards out of our own hand while giving partner the cheapest possible lane to shed one of their last two cards.

partnerBelief.summary: Partner (player 2) is near finish with only 2 cards. Their exact composition is unknown, but with such a short hand they likely hold low or medium singles they want to play rather than high control cards. They may pass on a low lead if they cannot beat it cleanly, or may play a slightly higher card to take the trick if it advances their finish.

opponentBelief.summary: Opponents (players 1 and 3) each have 3 cards and their holdings are completely unknown. They can almost certainly beat a 3 of diamonds, but at the opening of the game they have little incentive to commit high cards or trumps to win a trivial trick. They are more likely to pass and conserve strength unless one of them is also near finish.

actionRationale.primaryReason: Leading the lowest card (3 of diamonds) is the standard opening progression play. It starts emptying our hand with minimum strength, preserves the 4, 5 and 6 for subsequent tricks, and forces opponents to spend real value if they want to take control. It also gives partner the widest range of possible responses from their 2-card hand.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: Still a reasonable low lead, but it consumes the 4 and leaves only 5 and 6 as higher non-trump cards, slightly weakening future tricks with no compensating benefit.
Wastes a middle card from an already weak straight; reduces the number of cards that can reliably beat lower future leads.

Leading the highest available card sacrifices our best non-trump card for no advantage. A 6 is still easily beaten by opponents and does not protect partner or increase our chance of retaining the lead.

risks: An opponent may play a higher single and seize the lead.
Partner may be unable to respond, leaving us needing to recover the lead with a weak hand.
We break up a potential 3-4-5-6 straight, though no straight lead was available in the legal actions.
riskAssessment.mitigation: By leading the absolute lowest card we minimize the value opponents gain when they take the trick, and we keep our 4, 5 and 6 available to compete for future leads or cover partner's exit attempts.

notes: No public cards have been played, so all beliefs about opponent and partner holdings remain probabilistic. The hand-count signal (partner at 2 cards) is the only public information beyond the new_game event.
